coredata.dc:title: An efficient and stable narrow bandgap carbon dot-brookite composite over other CD-TiO2 polymorphs in rhodamine B degradation under LED light
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Oseghe, Ekemena O.
Msagati, Titus A.M.
Mamba, Bhekie B.
Ofomaja, Augustine E.
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon dot (CD) obtained from pine bark was loaded on three TiO2 polymorphs (anatase, rutile, and brookite abbreviated in this study as Ana, Rut, and Brk respectively) to evaluate their photocatalytic performance in rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible-LED light. The materials were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), x-ray powder diffraction (XRD), N2 sorption isotherm, and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV-diffused reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS), and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The x-ray result shows that the different pure pristine polymorphs and their composites were successfully prepared with Rut and CD-Rut composite having the highest crystallite size of 23.06 and 21.64 nm respectively. The N2 sorption isotherm of the materials shows Ana, Brk and their composite exhibited type IV isotherm with H series hysteresis loop, which implies that they are mesoporous materials. The isotherm exhibited by Rut and CD-Rut suggests that they are non-porous. However, their pore size distribution (PSD) shows that the materials possess mesopores that may have resulted from interparticle voids due to particle aggregation. Results from the photocatalytic degradation experiments showed that the materials performed in the following order CD-Brk>Brk>CD-Ana>CD-Rut>Rut>Ana with a corresponding percentage RhB removal of 99.91, 76.78, 36.28, 27.50, 22.65, and 12% respectively. The excellent performance of CD-Brk and Brk over the others is attributed to their narrow bandgap energy of 2.00 eV making them more active under the visible-LED light. CD-Brk performed better than Brk because it possesses more surface-active sites resulting from its relatively higher surface area. In terms of degradation mechanism, CD-Brk which was stable after 4 cycles of degradation, showed that superoxide anion radical (
O
2
• −
)
played major role in the degradation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 polymorphs
CD-TiO2 polymorph composite
Photodegradation
Visible LED light
Rhodamine B

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface functional groups and chemical states

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffractogram of the three polymorphs and their composites.
SEM micrograph for CD-Ana (a), CD-Rut (b), CD-Brk (c), and EDX spectrum for CD-Brk.
HRTEM micrograph and SAED (insert) of (a) CD with no SAED, (b) Ana, (c) CD-Ana, (d) CD-Brk, (e) CD-Rut.
N2 sorption isotherm and pore size distribution (insert) of the materials.
XPS spectra of CD-Brk composite for (a) Survey (b) Ti 2p (c) C 1s (d) O 1s.
UV-DRS spectra (a) and Tauc plot (b) of the materials.
Photocatalytic degradation of 200 mL RhB solution by the materials.
Effect of pH on the degradation of RhB the composite TiO2 polymorphs.
Reusability study of CD-Brk on the degradation of RhB.
Effect of scavengers on the degradation of RhB in the presence of CD-Brk.
Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB over CD-Brk.
Synthesis of pristine TiO2 polymorphs and CD-TiO2 polymorphs composite.
Textural, structural and optical properties of the materials.

head.ce:title: An efficient and stable narrow bandgap carbon dot-brookite composite over other CD-TiO2 polymorphs in rhodamine B degradation under LED light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa-Science Campus, Florida, 1710, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Applied and Computer Science, Vaal University of Technology, Vanderbijlpark Campus, Andries Potgieter, Boulevard, 1900, Vanderbijlpark, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Applied and Computer Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon dot (CD) obtained from pine bark was loaded on three TiO2 polymorphs (anatase, rutile, and brookite abbreviated in this study as Ana, Rut, and Brk respectively) to evaluate their photocatalytic performance in rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible-LED light. The materials were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), x-ray powder diffraction (XRD), N2 sorption isotherm, and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), UV-diffused reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS), and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The x-ray result shows that the different pure pristine polymorphs and their composites were successfully prepared with Rut and CD-Rut composite having the highest crystallite size of 23.06 and 21.64 nm respectively. The N2 sorption isotherm of the materials shows Ana, Brk and their composite exhibited type IV isotherm with H series hysteresis loop, which implies that they are mesoporous materials. The isotherm exhibited by Rut and CD-Rut suggests that they are non-porous. However, their pore size distribution (PSD) shows that the materials possess mesopores that may have resulted from interparticle voids due to particle aggregation. Results from the photocatalytic degradation experiments showed that the materials performed in the following order CD-Brk>Brk>CD-Ana>CD-Rut>Rut>Ana with a corresponding percentage RhB removal of 99.91, 76.78, 36.28, 27.50, 22.65, and 12% respectively. The excellent performance of CD-Brk and Brk over the others is attributed to their narrow bandgap energy of 2.00 eV making them more active under the visible-LED light. CD-Brk performed better than Brk because it possesses more surface-active sites resulting from its relatively higher surface area. In terms of degradation mechanism, CD-Brk which was stable after 4 cycles of degradation, showed that superoxide anion radical (
O
2
• −
)
played major role in the degradation process.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic pollutants such as pharmaceuticals [1], dyes [2], and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [3] are negatively impacting the aquatic systems since they are non-biodegradable and difficult to remove from wastewater. Treatment of wastewater have become a very important issue due to population explosion and rapid industrialization globally [4,5].

Among the various treatment technologies, TiO2 photocatalysis is considered the most promising technology due to its cost-effectiveness, availability, stability, and eco-friendliness [6]. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is basically defined by a number or combination of factors such as morphology, crystallite size, particle size, surface area, crystal structure, and porosity [7,8]. In most cases, these factors are influenced by the synthesis method [9]. Amongst these factors, much attention is given to the crystal structure of TiO2. TiO2 has three main polymorphs: anatase, brookite, and rutile [10,11]. Rutile is the most stable of all but less photoactive compared to anatase that has been widely studied. Most research on TiO2 photocatalysts has been on anatase, rutile or a combination of both in different percentages [12,13]. Brookite most times co-crystallizes with anatase, rutile or both and often makes separation difficult [10]. Hence it has been a less studied TiO2 polymorph in photocatalysis. Tran et al. [14], recently reported on the synthesis and photocatalytic application of highly active brookite in degrading hazardous organic compounds. Application of TiO2 in photocatalysis is faced with the problem of wide band gap energy and fast electron (e-) - hole (h+) recombination rate. This has resulted in the development of TiO2-based materials with enhanced photocatalytic activity in the visible region. The ability of TiO2-based materials to function under visible light is achieved by surface modification of TiO2 through loading, doping or co-doping with metal/non-metals [15].

Carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, graphitic carbon nitride, activated carbon, and carbon dot (CD) possesses exceptional properties for reducing the band gap energy and electron-hole recombination rate of anatase TiO2 [16–20]. Carbon dot in recent times have attracted attention in improving the performance of photocatalysts. This is due to its stability, robustness, biocompatibility, electron-accepting and transport properties [21–23]. Carbon dots have been synthesized through different techniques such as arc discharge [24] and laser ablation [25], hydrothermal carbonization [26], microwave [27] and pyrolysis [28]. Precursors for CD is either chemicals or biomass (natural resources) [22]. According to Tan et al. [29], it is more cost-effective and eco-friendly to produce CD from natural resources than from chemicals. Rai et al. [30], recently reported on the synthesis of fluorescence carbon dots from lignin which are also present in biomass. Pine is classified as a soft wood biomass containing about 45.6% cellulose, 24.0% hemicellulose, and 26.8% lignin [31], and therefore makes pine a potential source of CD. However, pine bark or pine cone has never been reported as precursors for the synthesis of CD.

Therefore, in this study, CD was prepared from pine bark and was loaded on the three different TiO2 polymorphs by hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic efficiency of both the pristine TiO2 polymorphs and CD/TiO2 polymorphs were evaluated by monitoring the degradation trend of Rhodamine B (RhB) under LED light. The purpose of the study was to obtain the most photocatalytic efficient pristine TiO2 polymorph and CD/TiO2 polymorph composite material since CD has only been loaded on other TiO2 polymorphs except brookite.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TIP) (C12H28O4Ti; 97%, Sigma Aldrich, CAS number 546-68-9), ethanol (≥95%) was purchased from associated chemical enterprises (ACE), acetic acid (CH3COOH; 99.5% LABCHEM), hydrochloric acid (HCl; 32% Merck), Rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3; Merck KGaA, CAS number 81-88-9), Pine bark (carbon dot precursor) was obtained from Vaal university of technology, Vanderbijlpark campus, South Africa.

Pine bark was washed with water, dried in the oven at 90 °C for 48 h and subsequently pulverized. Carbon dot was subsequently prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of the pulverized pine bark. Typically, 10 g of the pulverized pine bark was placed in a 100 mL Teflon cup containing 50 mL of ultrapure water and was transferred into a stainless steel-lined autoclave. The autoclave was tightly closed and kept in an oven set at 180 °C for 24 h. A dark green solution was obtained after allowing to cool, centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to separate the supernatant from the bulk residue particles. The clear solution was then freeze dried using a SCANVAC CoolSafe™ freeze dryer to obtain a highly water soluble and hygroscopic solid product.

The three polymorphs of TiO2 (anatase, brookite, and rutile) were synthesized from amorphous TiO2 by adapting methods of Tran et al. [14]. In preparing the amorphous TiO2, 10 g of TIP was added drop-wise into a 120 mL (ethanol and water in ratio 1: 2) solution while stirring. The solution was further stirred for 1 h and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min for separation. The obtained residue after separation was washed with ethanol, then dried at 60 °C overnight in the oven. TiO2 polymorphs were synthesized at different conditions such as acid types, acid concentrations, and temperature (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, 1 g of amorphous TiO2 was dispersed in 50 mL acidic solution by stirring for 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, heated in the oven at a certain temperature for 7 h. After heat treatment, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally and then the obtained residues were washed with deionize water to attain neutral pH and also with ethanol. The samples (Ana and Rut) were allowed to dry overnight in the oven at 60 °C. The Brk solution obtained after peptization was freeze dried using a SCANVAC CoolSafe™ freeze dryer to obtain to obtain an off-white powder.

The composite materials for the different TiO2 polymorphs were prepared separately (Scheme 1). TiO2 (Ana, Brk, or Rut) (1.35 g) and CD (0.15 g) were weighed into a 60 mL solution (ethanol and water in ratio 1: 2), sonicated for 10 min and stirred for 1 h. The sample after stirring was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 130 °C for 4 h and was allowed to cool naturally. The cooled sample was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed with ethanol, and dried overnight at 60 °C in the oven. The samples obtained where labelled as CD-Ana, CD-Brk, and CD-Rut corresponding to CD-anatase, CD-brookite, and CD-rutile respectively.

Scanning electron microscope attached to energy dispersive x-ray spectroscope (EDX) (ZEISS Ultra/Plus FEG-SEM) and transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2010) were used to obtain information on the morphology and elemental composition of the materials. The phase and crystalline size of the materials were determined by powder x-ray diffraction analysis (Shimadzu XRD-7000 X-ray diffractometer with a Cu K α radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm)). N2 sorption analysis on the samples were carried out using the ASAP 2020 micrometrics instrument after degassing under N2 flow at 90 °C for 1 h, and then increased to 200 °C for approximately 12 h. Fourier transmission infrared (FTIR) measurement was achieved using a Thermo Scientific (Nicolet iS50 FT-IR) instrument. UV-vis diffused reflectance measurement of the materials was done using a 2540 Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer with BaSO4 as a reference. The surface properties and binding energies of the samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo ESCAlab 250Xi) using monochromatic Al Kα (1486.7 eV) source.

Photocatalytic studies of the materials on RhB degradation were carried out in a quartz photochemical reactor with a 25 W flexible white vis-LED light strip (≈450 nm, 46 W/m2) as light source. Prior to irradiation, 250 mg/L of the catalyst was dispersed in a 200 mL RhB (5 mg/L) by sonicating for 5 min. The mixture was then stirred in the dark for 45 min to attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium before starting the photocatalytic experiment. At 20 min intervals, aliquot (2.5 mL) was sampled, immediately filtered using a 0.45 μm filter and the absorbance taken on a T80+ UV-vis spectrophotometer at 554 nm.

Fig. 1 shows the x-ray diffractograms of the three TiO2 polymorphs (anatase, brookite, and rutile) and their composites. It shows that all materials are polycrystalline with pure phases. According to the JCPDS database (Anatase- 00-021-1272; Rutile – 00-021-1276; Brookite – 00-029-1360), the diffractograms of the polymorphs and their composites shows peaks that are consistent with theoretical diffractions. All polymorphs apart from Brk showed an increase in the intensity of peaks when CD was loaded on them, suggesting that CD was deposited on the surface of Brk.

The crystallite size (D) of the materials was estimated using Scherrer's equation (eqn (1)).
(1)
D =
k λ
β C o s θ
where K (Scherrer's constant) ≈ 0.9, λ (wavelength of the X-ray) = 0.15406 nm, (full width at half maximum height (FWHM)) of the diffraction peaks, and is the peak diffraction angle. The and values were obtained from the (101), (120), and (110) reflections which corresponds to the anatase, brookite, and rutile phase respectively. The crystallite size of the materials is presented in Table 1 . It is observed that of the three TiO2 polymorphs, Brk had the lowest crystallite size (6.87 nm) while Rut had highest (23.04 nm). Different trend was observed in the CD- TiO2 polymorphs' composites as CD-Ana showed the lowest crystallite size (7.22 nm) while CD-Rut was the least. This implies that CD favours the reduction in the crystallite size of Ana and Rut TiO2 and the reverse is the case with Brk.

The FESEM micrographs of the CD/TiO2 polymorph composite materials are illustrated in Fig. 2 . It can be observed that CD-Ana (Fig. 2a) and CD-Brk (Fig. 2c) possess spherically shaped aggregated and agglomerated particle different from CD-Rut (Fig. 2b) which is rod-like in shape. No significant difference in the shapes of the CD/TiO2 polymorph composite and the pristine polymorphs (Fig. S1). Fig. 2d is the EDX spectrum of CD-Brk which gives information on the elemental composition of the material. It shows that the elements present in the samples are carbon, oxygen and titanium only. EDX spectra of other CD/TiO2 polymorph composite materials (Figs. S1d and S1e) shows similar elemental composition confirming their purity. HRTEM and SAED micrographs of the materials are presented in Fig. 2. Fig. 3 a demonstrates that the CD obtained from pine bark are quasi-spherical and mono-dispersed with an average diameter of 2.97 nm. This confirms that the carbonaceous material in this study is carbon dot since its diameter is less than 10 nm as defined by Jia et al. [32]. Ana (Fig. 3b) and CD-Ana (Fig. 3c) consists of agglomerated irregularly shaped particles of almost similar sizes with mean particle size of 9.12 nm each. Brk had an average particle size of 19.51 nm which decreased in the CD-Brk composite material (17.75 nm) as measured from the HRTEM micrographs in Fig. 3d and Fig. S2a. Rut and CD-Rut composite are quite different from the other TiO2 polymorphs as they both exhibit rod-shaped particle averaging 36.74 and 30.62 nm respectively in diameter.

Results of the SAED (Inserts of Fig. 3 and Fig. S2) validates the XRD results as the micrographs of CD-Ana and CD-Rut were more crystalline compared to the pristine ones. The reverse was the case with CD-Brk.

Nitrogen sorption analysis was carried out to investigate the textural properties of the materials. Fig. 4 shows the N2 sorption isotherms and the pore size distribution (PSD) (insert) for all the materials. According to IUPAC classification [33], all the materials apart from Rut and CD-Rut clearly exhibit the type IV adsorption isotherm with H series hysteresis loop. This implies that while Rut and CD-Rut possesses porosity that may have resulted from interparticle voids, the pores within other materials are mesoporous. Ana and CD-Ana possesses H1 hysteresis loop and therefore suggest that the pores are uniform hence the uni-modal distribution of pores with maximum appearing at 9.55 and 9.79 nm respectively as observed in insert of Fig. 4. Brk and CD-Brk exhibited H2 hysteresis and implies that the pores are not uniformly distributed hence the bi-modal PSD curve observed in the insert of Fig. 4. The BET surface area, BJH desorption pore volume and pore size of the materials are presented in Table 1. Anatase TiO2 (Ana) and its composite exhibited the highest surface area and pore volume while Rut and CD-Rut showed the least.

The difference in surface areas of the polymorphs and their composite are due to their particle sizes as observed from the TEM micrographs (Fig. 2 and S). Form the micrograph, Ana and its composite showed the least particle size and Rut and its composite exhibited the highest particle size. Hence the difference in surface area. Also, an improved surface area was observed when a composite of CD and the different TiO2 polymorphs were prepared compared to the pristine TiO2 polymorphs. This suggests that CD assisted in improving the surface area of the TiO2 polymorphs. Zhao et al. [34] also reported an enhanced surface area when CD was loaded on BiOBr. The BJH desorption pore size for all the composite materials except for CD-Brk decreased compared to the pristine TiO2 polymorphs. This implies that CD was deposited inside the pores of Ana and Rut, while the reverse was the case with Brk. This corroborate the XRD result were a decrease in the peak intensity of CD-Brk was attributed to deposition of CD on the surface of Brk.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functional groups and chemical states
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 5 is presented the FTIR spectra of the materials. Four notable bonding peaks at 3254, 1589, 1382, and 1043 cm−1 assigned to O-H, H2O, -COO-, and C-O respectively was observed in the CD spectrum. The pristine TiO2 polymorphs and their composites showed similar peaks ascribed to surface adsorbed H2O and O-H. Surface adsorbed H2O and O-H play key role in photocatalytic systems by reducing electron-hole recombination rate resulting in the formation of hydroxyl radicals (OH • ) [35]. It is interesting not to find -COO-, and C-O in the CD/TiO2 polymorph composite materials. This suggests an interaction between the polymorphs and CD to form the composite materials.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic analysis on CD-Brk was carried out to obtain information on the chemical composition and binding energies of the surface elements. In the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 6 a), 3 characteristic photoelectron peaks for carbon (C 1s), titanium (Ti 2p) and oxygen (O 1s) with binding energies of 284.8, 458.5 and 529.9 eV respectively were observed. The Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 6b) has two characteristic peaks at 458.5 eV and 464.2 eV which corresponds to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 respectively. The splitting between Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 is 5.7 eV which refers to the normal state of Ti4+ in CD-Brk [36]. Also, no peak for Ti3+ is observed in the Ti 2p3/2 and therefore implies that the main form of Ti in CD-Brk Ti4+. This was also reported by Zhang et al. [37]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6c) was deconvoluted and resulted in the identification of three peaks with binding energies of 284.8, 286.3, and 288.6 eV. The main peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to the C-C bond with sp2 orbital while the other peaks at 286.3, and 288.7 eV corresponds to C-O and O-C=O bonds respectively [36,38]. No peak typical for Ti-C bond was observed in any of the spectrum on Fig. 6 suggesting that the CD was not doped in TiO2 [37]. Fig. 6d also shows three characteristic peaks for the O 1s spectrum at 529.7 and 531.5, and 533.3 eV which are attributed to Ti-O, C-O, and C=O respectively [37,39].

The optical properties of the materials were studied by UV-diffuse reflectance spectroscopic (UV-DRS) measurement. The UV-DRS spectra of the materials are presented in Fig. 7 a. The pristine TiO2 polymorphs had significant change in the absorption edge (cut-off wavelength) in an increasing order of Ana < Rut < Brk. Similar trend is observed with the CD- TiO2 polymorphs. However, CD in the composite materials assisted in extending the absorption edge more into the visible region. Tauc plot, Fig. 7b, was used derived from the UV-DRS spectra of the materials and was used in estimating their direct band gap energy. Equation (2) shows the Tauc's relationship used in estimating the band gap energy of the materials.
(2)
( α h υ )
2
= A
( h υ −
E
g
)
where α = absorption coefficient,
h υ
= photon energy, A = constant, and Eg = bandgap energy. The bandgap energy is obtained by extrapolating the straight line of
(
α h υ
)
2
vs
h υ
plot (Fig. 7b). Table 1 presents the bandgap energies of the materials. It can be observed that Ana had the highest bandgap energy (3.20 eV as reported in literature [40]) which was reduced to 3.07 eV when CD was loaded on it (CD-Ana). The lowest bandgap energy was exhibited by Brk and CD-Brk (both 2.00 eV). Park et al. [41], also reported on the synthesis of oxygen-vacancy-modified brookite nanorod with band gap energies ranging from 2.35 to 3.34 eV. The result of the bandgap energy therefore suggests that Brk and CD-Brk should be more photoactive and energy efficient under visible light.

Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic activities of both the pristine TiO2 polymorphs and the CD-TiO2 polymorph composites in degrading RhB under vis-LED light irradiation. The photolysis experiment showed no obvious removal of RhB. A much improvement in the removal of RhB was observed in the presence of the photocatalysts. Amongst the photocatalysts, Ana, even with its high surface area, had the least percentage removal (12%). This is due to its relatively higher bandgap energy causing a very infinitesimal activation of the catalyst by the vis-LED light. The photocatalytic performance of Ana was enhanced (36.28%) upon loading with CD (CD-Ana) due to the slight decrease in its band gap energy (Table 1). Rut and CD-Rut exhibited bandgap energies of 3.04 and 3.00 eV relatively lower than Ana and CD-Ana, however, CD-Ana performed better than the CD-Rut. Rutile TiO2 has been reported to possess lower bandgap than anatase TiO2 but is a less efficient photocatalysts [42,43]. Also, even though the bandgap energy of CD-Rut is lower than that of CD-Ana, the surface area of CD-Rut is lower than CD-Ana in this study. This makes the number of active sites in CD-Rut less than CD-Ana. Hence the relatively lower photocatalytic performance of CD-Rut compared to CD-Ana. It is interesting to observe an excellent photocatalytic performance of Brk (76.78%) and CD-Brk (99.91%) in RhB degradation under vis-LED light. The percentage removal of RhB by CD-Brk is higher than Brk due to the difference in their surface area (Table 1) giving CD-Brk more active sites for the reaction. It is generally observed in this study that the presence of CD in the different TiO2 polymorphs assisted in improving their band gap energies and surface area which invariably improved their photocatalytic performance. Zhang et al. [44] also reported an improved photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of organic pollutant by carbon dot/TiO2 composite over pristine TiO2.

The effect of pH on the photodegradation of RhB (5 mg/L) by the composite materials was investigated. The pH considered for this study were 4, 7, and 9. Fig. 9 shows the result of the degradation of RhB by the composite materials after 120 min. CD-Brk exhibited excellent performance more than CD-Ana and CD-Rut at the various pH conditions. However, 100% degradation of RhB by CD-Brk was recorded at pH 4 and 7. A decrease in the percentage degradation (69.4%) of RhB was observed at pH 9. The point of zero charge (PZC) for TiO2 ranges from 6.5 to 6.8 [45] and considering the small amount of CD (10%) in the composite, there will be little or no change in the PZC of the CD-Brk. Therefore, at pH 9, the surface of the composite material is negatively charged. Also, at basic pH, the carboxyl group of RhB is deprotonated [46,47] thereby leading to repulsion between the deprotonated CD-Brk and RhB. Hence a reduction in the photocatalytic efficiency of CD-Brk at pH 9.

Reusability studies of CD-Brk for the photodegradation of RhB was carried out to evaluate its photostability. The RhB and catalyst concentrations were 5 mg/L (200 mL at pH = 7) and 300 mg/L respectively. Fig. 10 shows the outcome of the experiment after 120 min. It is observed that after four cycles, the photocatalytic activity of CD-Brk decreased slightly by only 10%. The decrease in activity might be due to slight loss in CD-Brk after each cycle of the experiment suggesting that the composite material is stable and reusable.

To elucidate the mechanism for the photodegradation RhB by CD-Brk, several photocatalytic experiments in the presence and absence of scavengers were carried out. The scavengers employed for the experiments were potassium iodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA), tert-butanol, and benzoquinone (BQ) for scavenging h+, OH•, e-, and O2 •- respectively. Fig. 11 shows the effect of scavengers on the percentage degradation of RhB in the presence of CD-Brk. In the absence of a scavenger, the percentage degradation was 100 after 120 min.

A drop in percentage degradation was observed in the presence of scavengers indicating that all the reactive species played a role in the degradation of RhB. However, O2 •- played major role in the percentage degradation of RhB as only 11.01% degradation was recorded after 120 min of the experiment. Results from this study agrees with reports from Hao et al. (2017) [48] and Lu et al. (2018) [20]. Where they showed that O2 •- is key in the degradation of RhB in the presence of carbonaceous/TiO2 composite materials.
Based on the scavenging experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism is proposed in Fig. 12 .

Summary of the mechanism is presented in the equations (3)–(9). Typically, when CD-Brk is exposed to visible LED light source, electron (e-) is excited from the valence band (VB) of Brk into the conduction band (CB) thereby leaving a hole (h+) in the VB. CD in the composite act as e- sink resulting in better separation of the photogenerated charge pairs.
(3)
C D − B r k + h υ
→
e
C B
−

2
O

The electron on the CD reacts with O2 in the solution to form superoxide anion (O2 •-) which is undergoes further reactions (equations (5)–(7)) to form hydroxyl radical (OH•). The h+ on the VB of TiO2, would either react with the surface adsorbed water or hydroxyl group to form OH• (equation (8)). However, most importantly is the fact that all the reactive species are all involved in the degradation of RhB as is seen in equation (9).

In this study, pine bark derived carbon dot (CD) and the three pure TiO2 polymorphs (Ana, Rut, and Brk) were successfully prepared. Also, composite samples of CD on the three different TiO2 polymorphs (CD-Ana, CD-Rut, CD-Brk) were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The XRD results shows that while the CD improved the crystallinity of Ana and Rut, the reverse was the case with Brk. Also observed was an improvement in the BET surface area of the TiO2 polymorphs due to modification by CD. Of all the prepared materials, Brk and CD-Brk performed best in RhB removal under vis-LED light compared to the other pristine and composite TiO2 polymorphs. Their unique performance is attributed to their relatively narrower bandgap energy of about 2.00 eV. CD-Brk had BET surface area of 96.75 m2/g which is higher than that of Brk (89.42 m2/g) and therefore implies higher active sites on CD-Brk for the degradation of RhB. CD-Brk demonstrated 100% RhB removal at pH 3 and 7 and later dropped to 69.4% at pH 9. Results from the scavenging experiment suggests that the photocatalytic performance of CD-Brk which was stable after 4 cycles of RhB degradation was majorly controlled by the superoxide anion radical (O2 •-).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the Department of Chemistry, the Research Directorate, Vaal University of Technology, and Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability Research Unit, University of South Africa for supporting this project with finance and research facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
SupplementaryInformation SupplementaryInformation

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2019.04.121.
